sop.coo_evaluation:
# SOP Audit & Build Plan for The Forge

**Gaps — no SOP yet**

- **Member complaints & incident reporting** – no documented escalation path for conflicts, injuries, safeguarding concerns, or complaints. Critical for duty of care & insurance.
- **Financial controls & reconciliation** – no SOP for monthly P&L review, cash handling, refund approvals, or fraud prevention (who approves what spend?).
- **Staffing handover & absence cover** – no process for shift swaps, sickness cover, or onboarding temporary/relief coaches when permanent staff unavailable.
- **Health & safety inspections** – no documented schedule for equipment checks, fire drills, deep cleans, or hazard audits (beyond facilities maintenance).
- **Member win-back & churn prevention** – no SOP for identifying at-risk members, pause/freeze protocols, or reactive re-engagement campaigns.
- **Coach performance reviews & capability management** – no documented feedback cycle, observation frequency, or improvement plan process.
- **Class capacity & waitlist management** – no SOP for how many members per class, how waitlists feed demand planning, or how to add classes.
- **Cancellation & rescheduling** – member-facing SOP for refunds, class cancellations, gym closures, and make-ups.

**Needs updating**

- **Client onboarding (22 SOPs)** – likely missing post-Day 1 check-ins (Day 7, Day 14) and induction video sign-off proof; no SOP linking induction completion to class booking eligibility.
- **Coach onboarding (17 SOPs)** – no documented probation review gate (30/60/90-day checkpoints) or what triggers progression to "fully approved" status.
- **Marketing (10/12)** – Email Newsletter &

kpi.advice:
# THE FORGE — FRACTIONAL CFO BRIEF

## INCREASE REVENUE

- **AR/M collapse is the red flag.** Down from £170.43 (Dec) to £120.48 (June)—clients paying less per month. Push *class packages* (10-class blocks at £180 vs. £20/drop-in) and *PT add-ons* (single sessions at £35–45). June's 147 active clients should yield £175+/month average, not £120.
- **Churn went silent in June (0%) but revenue fell £2.5k vs May.** You've stabilized retention but *lost paying members*—attrition likely hit in early June. Reactivation sprint needed: email lapsed members with a 2-week free trial or "comeback offer" (£25 for first month). May had 122 active; June has 147 but lower ARPU suggests freebies/discounts used to recruit.
- **December peaked at £21k on 124 members; June is £20k on 147 members.** The bigger studio *should* unlock volume. Plug underbooked classes with a "5am/off-peak discount" tier (£40/month) to fill the new capacity without cannibalizing full-price £60–70 memberships.
- **Capitalize on zero June churn.** Launch a referral scheme: £30 credit per referred friend who joins. Cost of acquisition is low if existing members do the work.

## REDUCE EXPENSES

- **Non-people expenses are out of control.** May £9,233 → June £9,390—flat despite bigger studio settled and April fit-out done. Breakdown needed: if it's rent, utilities, insurance, or software subscriptions, renegotiate. Target: drop to £7,500/month by Q3 (you were at £4,303 in Nov before the move).
- **People costs rising (May £3,550 → June £3,686) while ARPU falls.** June payroll is 20.8% of revenue; May was 17.6%. Hire isn't justified by the revenue drop. Audit: are you double-staffed post-move or paying moving bonuses? Freeze recruitment; use casual/sessional instructors for class overflow instead. Target: hold at £3,400/month (18.3% of £18.5k projected revenue).

## IMPROVE PROFITABILITY & TAX

- **Profit margin tanked: May 29.3% → June 5.28%. That's the core issue.** Non-people costs spi

staff.advice:
• **Move Grace's Build: Lower (17 classes) to JoJo.** JoJo is running 133 sessions and has the technical strength; Grace stepping back means this is the priority handover. Build Lower is core curriculum and shouldn't rest on the founder.

• **Redistribute Grace's Build: Upper (9 classes) and Elevate (6 classes) to Eilis.** Eilis is under-loaded at 48 sessions and can grow into these. This reduces Grace to zero classes and gives Eilis meaningful progression toward full-time capacity.

• **Assign Grace's Strongevity (3 classes) and No Sweat Intro (3 classes) to JoJo or Eilis as backups.** These are lower-barrier offerings; either coach can cover them, reducing single-point-of-failure risk on the founder.

• **JoJo's 44 PT sessions are legitimate load.** Don't reduce them, but ensure Eilis picks up more 1:1 work (she has only 11) so PT isn't dependent on one person and Eilis develops client relationships.

• **Ignore the 40 Body Composition Scans combined.** They're admin, not coaching. Distribute them across whoever has the timetable space that month, or train admin staff to handle them—they free up real coaching time.

• **Result: Grace drops to zero delivery; JoJo stays ~150 sessions (sustainable head coach load); Eilis grows to ~70+ sessions, building depth in Build and Elevate, plus more PT.**

class_stats.suggestion:
**For quiet studio time between PT sessions and classes:**

1. Add a 30-minute paid "Open Lift" slot on Thursday and Monday evenings — members often want unstructured time to work on their own lifts between the structured Build classes, and this captures demand without competing with scheduled classes.

2. Run a monthly "Barbell Clinic" workshop (60 mins) in the quiet Friday afternoon slot — Barbelle is severely underattended (12 in last 30 days), so a one-off skills session focused on form and technique will build confidence and feed members into the regular class.

**To lift the quieter classes themselves:**

3. Move Engine from its current slot to a Tuesday or Wednesday morning — it has only 15-52 attendees, suggesting it's either poorly timed or unknown; repositioning to a busier day gives it visibility and captures members already in-gym for Build classes, then offer the first 5 attendees a free smoothie to build habit.

kpis.Action: INTERNAL: Create clear service menu (for coaches and for members) — what we offer, what it costs, what's included.
kpis.advice:
# Service Menu Execution for The Forge

**1. Start with your *actual* revenue data, not wishes**
Pull last 3 months of transactions. What are members *actually* buying? (Classes vs. PT vs. packages?) What's your highest-margin offering? Build the menu around what's working, not what sounds good. This shapes pricing logic.

**2. Create two *separate* documents — coaches see cost/margin, members see benefits**
Coach version: [Service] → Cost to deliver → Your margin → Upsell hooks (e.g., "Package holders upgrade to PT 60% of the time"). Member version: [Service] → What problem it solves → Social proof/results → Price. Never show coaches' margins to members; never hide true inclusions from coaches.

**3. Test pricing with 5 current members before finalizing**
Grab coffee with your most loyal/chatty members. Show them draft pricing on 2-3 service tiers. Listen for hesitation — that's your real price ceiling. One conversation beats guessing.

**4. Lock in what's *included* in each tier ruthlessly**
Vagueness kills upsells. Example: "Unlimited classes" is clear. "Access to community" is not. For each service tier, write: class types + frequency, PT sessions (if any), app access, gear discounts, guest passes — specific numbers only.

**5. Make coaches' version actionable for selling TODAY**
Include a one-page "quick pitch" per service. Coaches shouldn't hunt for what to say. E.g., "New member? Start with: 'Four weeks unlimited + one free PT assessment = £X. No lock-in.'"

kpis.Action: EXTERNAL: Refine our funnel landing pages to make it very clear what we offer, look at common questions
kpis.advice:
# Landing Page Execution for The Forge

**1. Audit your current drop-off points first**
Pull your analytics right now — where do visitors leave? (Homepage? Pricing page? Sign-up form?) Record the exact pages. Then grab 5-10 members and ask: "What confused you before joining?" Their answers trump your assumptions. Focus refinement on the actual leak, not what you think is broken.

**2. Lead with the women-only angle, hard**
Your USP isn't "fitness" — it's safety, community, no judgment. Your landing page headline should say this in the first 5 seconds. Test copy like: "Strength Training Built for Women. No Ego. No Mirrors. No Awkward." Then immediately answer: *Why does women-only matter to your audience?*

**3. Steal your members' own words for FAQs**
Don't write generic Q&As. Instead: review your Insta DMs, emails, and WhatsApp chats for actual questions people ask. That's your FAQ. Screenshot the real questions, add short answers. Update weekly with new ones that come in. This speaks member language, not corporate speak.

**4. Create a one-pager "What You Get" visual**
One simple graphic showing: class types, what results to expect (strength gains, confidence, community), membership tiers, and next step. Link this on every landing page. People scan, not read — give them the scannable version.

**5. A/B test one element this week**
Change your CTA button from "Sign Up" to "Start Free Week" or test a different headline. Run both for 5-7 days. Measure clicks and sign-ups

kpis.Action: Implement internal member feedback system (simple monthly form or post-class check-in)
kpis.advice:
# Internal Feedback System — Execution for The Forge

• **Start with post-class captures, not forms.** Use a simple clipboard rotation at peak times (evenings/weekends) — ask 2-3 specific questions while members are still in the mindset (What worked? What didn't? Would you recommend this class?). Forms get ignored; verbal + quick note takes 60 seconds and gets 3x response rate.

• **Make it member-led, not admin-heavy.** Assign rotating "feedback champions" (2-3 keen members per week) to chat with 5-6 others during cool-down. They report back to you in a Slack message or quick call. This builds community AND keeps you from drowning in admin while giving members ownership.

• **Tie feedback directly to decisions she makes.** If 4 members say they want 6am classes, announce *that week* that you're testing one. Members see action = they keep feeding back. One member complaint about music? Change it visibly. This closes the loop and proves it matters.

• **Monthly digest, not constant noise.** Spend 30 mins on the last Friday of each month categorizing patterns (class preferences, pain points, retention risks). Share top 3 learnings with your team + a "here's what we're changing" note to members. Keeps momentum without overwhelm.

• **Measure what actually moves the needle.** Track retention rate by member cohort *before/after* implementing this. If feedback system doesn't correlate to less churn or higher class attendance within 2 months, redesign it. Data tells you if it's working.

kpis.Action: Write SOP's for membership members, personal training members, fuel forward, well75, strongevity. Every service should have a clear SOP.
kpis.advice:
# SOP Execution Strategy for The Forge

**1. Assign one person as "SOP owner" — not a committee**
Grace or a trusted manager owns this end-to-end. Give them 2-3 hours weekly for 6 weeks. Committees create endless revision loops. One decision-maker = done faster.

**2. Start with your *actual* processes, not ideal ones**
Shadow your best staff member delivering each service for one shift. Write down exactly what they do (not what the manual says they *should* do). This grounds SOPs in reality and gets buy-in from your team.

**3. Use a dead-simple template: Problem → Step → Outcome**
For each service, create a one-pager maximum:
- **What triggers this SOP** (e.g., "new membership sign-up")
- **Each step in order** (who, what, when, how long)
- **What success looks like** (e.g., "member has access code + first class booked within 24 hours")

Skip flowcharts. They're pretty but useless operationally.

**4. Batch-write by service type, not randomly**
Group the "similar friction" SOPs together (Membership + Well75 share onboarding logic; PT + Strongevity share assessment logic). This reduces repetition and spots inconsistencies fast.

**5. Stress-test with a real staff member TODAY**
Give your SOP owner's draft to your newest team member Friday afternoon. Have them follow it step-by-step without asking questions. Where they get stuck = what to rewrite.

**Timeline: Draft done by end of week 2.**

kpis.Action: Establish a consistent social media content calendar with 3–4 posts/week planned in advance
kpis.advice:
# Social Media Content Calendar Execution for The Forge

**1. Use a template, not a tool (yet)**
Start with a simple Google Sheet: rows = weeks, columns = Mon/Wed/Fri/Sat. Fill 8 weeks at once during one 90-minute session. Content types: member transformations (60%), behind-the-scenes/coach tips (25%), event promos (15%). This removes daily decision-making and takes 15 mins/week to batch-create content.

**2. Assign one person as "keeper" with a backup**
Grace or one trusted staff member owns uploading by 7am on post days. Give them a 30-second checklist (hashtags: #BelfastWomen #TheForgeGym, caption tone, link if promo). Backup prevents the calendar dying when life happens.

**3. Repurpose relentlessly**
One photoshoot = 8+ posts. Capture 15 mins of video during class, chop into 4 clips (form tips, member wins, energy, coach feature). Photograph transformation stories once, use across 2-3 posts over a month. Cut your content creation work by 60%.

**4. Lock in a content gathering rhythm**
Every Tuesday: ask coaches for one "tip of the week." Every second Thursday: grab 3 member stories (ask during cool-down). These inputs feed the calendar—you're not scrambling for ideas.

**5. Track what actually moves women through the door**
After 4 weeks, note which posts got DMs or inquiries (use Linktree or a landing page link). Double down on *that* content type next quarter.

kpis.Action: Schedule regular videography sessions every 8 weeks with Jordan
kpis.advice:
# Executing Your Videography Schedule with Jordan

**Lock it in NOW, not later:**
- Book all 8-week slots for the next 12 months TODAY (6 sessions minimum). Put them in both calendars with automatic reminders 2 weeks prior. Momentum kills when you're rescheduling—treat it like a non-negotiable client commitment.

**Pre-plan what you're shooting to avoid wasted sessions:**
- 2 weeks before each shoot, have a brief list ready: member testimonial (film 2-3), class highlight reel segment, new equipment demo, or behind-the-scenes moment. Send to Jordan 7 days out so he knows the vibe. Vague briefs = bloated editing time = delays.

**Create one clear content calendar it feeds:**
- Decide upfront WHERE these videos land: Instagram Reels (monthly), email campaigns, Google Business Profile, website homepage rotation. This forces you to structure shoots around what actually drives inquiries—not just "content for content's sake."

**Tie it to a specific revenue metric:**
- Track which videography-generated content drives member inquiries. After 3 shoots, audit which platform/video type converted best, then bias future shoots toward that. Otherwise you're just paying for nice videos, not growth.

**Build micro-content extraction into the brief:**
- Ask Jordan to deliver not just one polished video, but 5-8 short clips (15-30 sec) from the same shoot you can repurpose. One shoot = one month of social media fuel if structured right.

What's your biggest bottleneck—finding time to brief Jordan, or knowing what content actually converts for membership sales?

kpis.Action: Revisit systemize automations for 6 week trial enquiries
kpis.advice:
# Systemizing Trial Enquiry Automations for The Forge

**1. Audit the current leakage first**
Pull your last 50 trial enquiries and map exactly where each one drops off (initial contact → booking → first session → conversion). You probably lose 30-40% between enquiry and showing up. Fix the biggest leak before adding automation layers.

**2. Build a three-touch sequence with *specific* friction removal**
- **Touch 1 (within 2 hours):** Auto-reply confirming receipt + direct link to book trial slot (not a form, direct calendar booking). Friction killer: no back-and-forth emails.
- **Touch 2 (day 2 if not booked):** Personal message from Grace or a team member—"I noticed you haven't booked yet, here's why women choose us" with testimonial video (45 sec max, emotional, not corporate).
- **Touch 3 (day 5):** Reminder + sweeten the deal—"Trial includes a free consultation with our trainer about your goals."

**3. Pre-trial automation (the conversion lever)**
Day before their session: SMS reminder + "What to bring" checklist + photo of the actual studio space they'll use (removes intimidation). Day-of: friendly text 2 hours before = no-shows drop 40%.

**4. Capture feedback *during* trial with a micro-survey**
Post-session automated text with 3 questions: "How was it?" (emoji scale) + "What surprised you?" (open) + "What's your biggest fitness goal?" Auto-tags responses—this feeds your sales conversation.

**5. Track only what changes the dial

kpis.Action: Finalise and document weekly action checklist for each role (JoJo, Eilis, Sarah) with KPIs they own
kpis.advice:
# Weekly Action Checklist + KPI Ownership — Execution Plan

• **Map backwards from revenue/retention first.** Don't assign tasks then find KPIs. For each person (JoJo/Eilis/Sarah), pick ONE primary KPI they directly impact (e.g., JoJo = new member sign-ups, Eilis = class attendance %, Sarah = member retention rate). Build their checklist around moving that number.

• **Make checklists brutally specific to their actual job.** Not "manage social media" — "post 2x Instagram Stories Mon/Wed/Fri by 6pm, tag 3 existing members, measure saves + shares." Each task should have a time, frequency, and measurable output they can't fudge.

• **Embed the KPI review into the checklist itself.** Add a Friday review line: "Check [their KPI] against last week's target. Document variance." They see the connection between actions and results in real-time — no mystery.

• **Use a shared, live doc (Google Sheet or Notion).** Not printed or emailed. JoJo, Eilis, and Sarah update their own rows weekly. You (Grace) see status without chasing. This forces accountability and gives you early warning when someone's slipping.

• **Start with a 2-week pilot.** Run this with one person first. Learn what's actually doable vs. what's wishful thinking. Then roll out to the other two with real data on what worked.

kpis.Action: Run team vision session — share the SSO, 90-day plan, and each person's lane clearly
kpis.advice:
# Team Vision Session Execution for Grace

**Before you run it:**
- **Lock in 90 mins max, in-person if possible.** Remote kills the energy you need. Book a slot where people aren't knackered (not end of Friday). Send the SSO and 90-day plan to the team 48 hours early — *read it first, don't wing it*.

**During the session — this is the structure that works:**
- **Open with "why this matters to YOU personally"** (5 mins). Not the business pitch — the real reason. "If we hit X, we can hire Y" or "This protects your shifts/pay." People own what they see affects them.
- **Walk the SSO section by section, pause after each, ask "what questions kill this for you?"** Listen hard. If three people are confused about the same thing, you explained it badly — fix it live.
- **Then the 90-day plan as a story, not a list.** "We're doing ABC because it feeds into XYZ by April." Connect the dots for them.

**The "lane" bit — make it specific AND visible:**
- Assign one owner per 90-day initiative (not diffused). Write names next to goals on a board they can see. "Sarah owns new member retention. Mark owns the referral program." Own it = care about it.
- Ask each person: "What do you need from me (or from each other) to actually crush this?" Write those dependencies down and review them in 2 weeks.

**Leave them with one thing to do before they leave** — even if it's just texting you one idea for their lane. Engagement kills ambiguity.

kpis.Action: Audit workload distribution — identify who is over-stretched and rebalance over first 30 days
kpis.advice:
# Workload Audit & Rebalancing for The Forge

• **Map actual hours NOW, not perceived load** — Have each team member (instructors, desk staff, cleaners, managers) log *actual* time spent on each activity for 5 working days. You'll spot the real bottlenecks (e.g., one person handling all member onboarding, social media bleeding into class prep). Perception-based decisions fail.

• **Identify the "single points of failure" first** — Find the 1-2 tasks only one person can do. These are your rebalancing priorities. If only one instructor knows the free-weights coaching or one person runs all member communications, that's where your stretched person is. Cross-train the second-most capable person immediately on these.

• **Create a "task swap" not a "role swap"** — Don't reorganize job titles in 30 days. Instead, strip out 2-3 specific tasks from the over-stretched person and assign them to someone with bandwidth. Example: If your manager is drowning in class scheduling *and* member complaints, move scheduling to your most organized instructor and handle it in a structured 1-hour weekly block.

• **Use the first week to *decide*, week 2-3 to *pilot*, week 4 to *embed*** — Week one: audit + identify. Week two: trial the new assignments with the over-stretched person available as backup. Week four: lock it in. This prevents panic-driven mistakes.

• **Track one metric weekly** — Measure the stretched person's "unfinished tasks at close-of-day." If that number drops by >40% in 30 days, rebalancing worked. If not

# Cost Reduction Playbook for The Forge

**Audit your fixed costs first (30 mins today)**
- Pull your last 3 months of bank statements. Rank every recurring expense by size. Most gyms leak 15-20% on rent, utilities, insurance, software subscriptions, and cleaning contracts. Grace likely has 2-3 quick wins here before touching operations.

**Renegotiate vendor contracts NOW—not in 6 months**
- Call your cleaning company, insurance broker, and equipment supplier TODAY. Say: "We're reviewing providers—what can you do to keep our business?" Most will cut 8-12% just to avoid losing you. Stagger these calls across a week so each feels urgent.

**Shift one operational expense to variable**
- Instead of full-time cleaning staff, move to per-shift contracted cleaners or negotiate a hybrid model. This alone could save 4-6% immediately. Same logic for PT staff—encourage more independent contractors on revenue-share versus hourly.

**Cut the 20% least-used membership perks or amenities**
- Track which included services members actually use. If only 5% use the smoothie bar or WiFi lounge, eliminate it or make it premium. Redirect that budget spend to what drives retention (class quality, equipment).

**Target 10% through member experience, not cuts that damage it**
- Slight price increase on premium tiers (3-5%) + push 10% of casual members to annual upfront billing (you get cash flow, they lock in). Keeps member satisfaction high while hitting your margin target.

# Updating Monthly Finance KPIs for The Forge

**1. Automate the data pull first**
Stop manual spreadsheet updates. Set up a simple integration (Zapier, Power Automate, or your gym software's native export) to pull membership revenue, churn rate, and class attendance directly into a shared Google Sheet by the 1st of each month. 15 mins to set up saves you 2 hours monthly.

**2. Focus on the 3 metrics that actually drive decisions**
Track: (a) Monthly recurring revenue per active member, (b) Churn rate month-on-month, (c) Class utilisation rate. Ignore vanity metrics. These three tell you if growth is real or if you're just adding members who quit in 90 days.

**3. Front-load variance analysis**
The moment you update figures, compare vs. last month AND same month last year. Note *why* changes happened (e.g., "churn spiked 3% — was it the pricing change or seasonal?"). This takes 10 mins but stops you guessing later.

**4. Pair KPIs to one action each**
Don't just eyeball numbers. If MRR is flat, that KPI triggers a review of your referral programme. If churn is up, that triggers a retention audit. Make it a rule: KPI update = automatic next move identified. Otherwise it's theatre.

**5. Share one insight, not the whole sheet**
Update Grace monthly with one paragraph: "Utilisation hit 78% — we can add a class without risk; here's the revenue impact." Your team doesn't need a dashboard; they need clarity on what changes next week.

# Financial Forecasting for The Forge — Execution Plan

• **Start with 12-month rolling forecast, not annual budgets** — Build a simple spreadsheet tracking: membership revenue (by tier), class pack sales, PT sessions, and fixed costs (rent, utilities, staff). Update monthly with actual vs. projected. This catches cash flow gaps 8-10 weeks ahead when you can still fix them.

• **Segment revenue by membership lifecycle** — Forecast separately for: new members (expect 30-40% churn first 3 months), retained members, and reactivations. Women's-only gyms have different retention curves than generic gyms — know yours by pulling 6 months of membership data now.

• **Scenario-plan for Q4 and January** — Women's fitness sees predictable spikes Jan 2-31 and summer holidays (July-Aug dips). Build three forecasts: conservative (-20% members), expected, and growth (+15% PT uptake). Know exactly which cost to cut if January underperforms.

• **Get 3 months of historical data into a single document TODAY** — Don't wait for perfect systems. Grab bank statements, membership platform exports, and PT booking data. Messy data beats no data. This becomes your baseline for "is the forecast working?"

• **Decide one KPI to forecast religiously** — Pick either: monthly recurring revenue (MRR) or member count. Choose one, forecast it weekly, compare to actual every Friday. That discipline is what makes forecasting actually guide decisions instead of sitting in a spreadsheet.

What membership data do you have readily accessible right now?

kpis.Action: Map profitability of each service (classes vs PT vs add-ons) — build a simple per-service P&L
kpis.advice:
# Profitability Mapping for The Forge — Execution Blueprint

**Get your actual numbers in one spreadsheet THIS WEEK:**
- Pull 90 days of revenue from your payment processor/till (class bookings, PT sessions, retail/supplements). Don't estimate. Then extract fixed costs you can reasonably allocate: facility rent, utilities, insurance split by service type (PT takes more heating/AC time). Variable costs matter most: instructor pay per class, PT trainer commission, product cost-of-goods. Use last month's actuals, not guesses.

**Focus your P&L on the ONE number that matters — contribution margin per service:**
- Revenue minus ONLY direct variable costs (what actually changes if you run one more class vs. one fewer). Classes might be 70% margin (£300 revenue, £90 instructor cost). PT might be 40% margin if you're paying 50% commission. This shows what's actually funding your fixed costs, not vanity profit percentages.

**Rank services by contribution margin per *hour of facility use*:**
- A 1-hour class generating £200 margin beats a 1-hour PT session generating £150 margin because space is your real constraint. This reveals if you're wasting premium slot times on lower-efficiency offerings.

**Run a 30-second "breakeven reality check":**
- If classes are losing money (they won't be, but check), kill them or reprice. If PT is only 35% margin, you've found your pricing problem immediately — raise rates on new clients this month while grandfathering existing ones.

**Act on ONE insight in the next 14 days:**
- Don't build a perfect model. Use this to either reduce a

kpis.Action: Audit all active systemize automations — remove duplicates, confirm enquiry-to-trial flow works end to end.
kpis.advice:
# Execution Plan for Automation Audit

**1. Map the entire enquiry-to-trial journey in 90 minutes**
- List every touchpoint: website form → email → SMS → calendar invite → welcome sequence
- Document *which tool* handles each step (Zapier, native CRM, email platform, etc.)
- Identify where data passes between systems — this is where duplicates hide

**2. Find duplicates ruthlessly**
- Run a test enquiry through your system yourself — watch for multiple welcome emails or calendar invites hitting you
- Check your CRM for duplicate contact records created in the last 3 months (automation sign)
- Search your email sequences for overlapping triggers (e.g., two automations firing on "new lead" when only one should)

**3. Kill the broken links**
- Test: Can someone actually book a trial from email click? Does the calendar sync back to your system?
- Check if trial confirmations are reaching people consistently (ask recent trial members)
- Verify payment/membership automations trigger *after* first session, not before

**4. Audit for revenue leaks**
- How many enquiries become trials? Should be tracking this weekly — if it's under 70%, your automation is broken or your nurture sequence sucks
- Check if low-show trial rates point to missing reminder automations

**5. Document decisions & assign one owner**
- Delete/consolidate duplicates today (don't schedule it)
- Assign yourself or one team member as "automation owner" — prevents future chaos

**Time: 4-5 hours over two days. ROI: Likely 10-15% improvement in trial conversion from fixes alone.**

kpis.Action: Pick 3 repetitive tasks and build an AI automation
kpis.advice:
# AI Automation for The Forge: 3 Tasks to Automate NOW

**Task 1: Membership Inquiry Responses**
- Use ChatGPT or Zapier to auto-reply to booking form submissions with pricing, class schedule, and a trial offer link within 2 minutes (capture 30-40% who abandon before hearing back)
- Template: injury concerns → suggested beginner classes; pricing questions → tier comparison + "book a call" CTA
- **Time saved:** 5-8 hours/month, eliminates "slow to respond" objections

**Task 2: Class Cancellation Notifications & Waitlist Management**
- Build a simple Zapier workflow: When Grace marks a class "cancelled" in her booking system → auto-SMS/email members + auto-text waitlist members with rebooking options
- **Cuts admin friction in half**, reduces angry members who show up to cancelled slots

**Task 3: Monthly Progress Check-ins (90-day retention)**
- Use HubSpot or Make (formerly Integromat) to trigger automated "how's your progress?" SMS on day 30/60/90 post-signup, with survey link asking about goals + satisfaction
- Auto-flag disengaged members (low class bookings) for Grace's 1:1 save conversation
- **Impact:** Catch churn before it happens; turn data into 1-on-1 touchpoints that feel personal

**Critical move:** Pick the ONE that costs you the most time THIS WEEK. Automate that first. Prove ROI. Then scale.

kpis.Action: Start Cyber security for forge website
kpis.advice:
# Cyber Security Execution for The Forge Website

• **Audit first, then fix** — Run a free security scan via Sucuri or Qualys to identify actual vulnerabilities (outdated plugins, weak SSL, exposed databases). Don't pay for solutions until you know what's broken. Takes 30 mins, costs £0.

• **SSL certificate non-negotiable** — If you're collecting payment info or member data, you need HTTPS. If your host doesn't include it free, upgrade hosts (most do now). This is table-stakes for a gym taking card details. Budget: £0-30/year.

• **Password + 2FA on admin accounts** — Your WordPress/website login is a theft target. Enforce strong passwords and enable two-factor authentication via Wordfence plugin (free). One staff member's weak password = your entire member database compromised.

• **Monthly backup automation** — Set automatic daily backups to cloud storage (UpdraftPlus plugin, ~£70/year). If you get hacked, you restore in hours not weeks. This is your insurance policy.

• **Assign one person accountability** — Pick a staff member or delegate to your web host's support team for quarterly security reviews. Without an owner, it drifts. 15 mins per quarter = protected business.

**Reality check:** Most gyms get targeted by credential-stuffing bots looking for payment info, not sophisticated hackers. These steps block 95% of attacks. Act this week.

marketing.advice:
**Insights**

Your cost-per-lead has ballooned 350% from April to July (£6 → £27) whilst lead volume has collapsed 94% (69 → 4). July's CPL is unsustainable—at £27/lead you're only profitable because close rate spiked to 75%, but that's noise in a sample of 4. The real story is your audience is exhausted, creative is stale, or targeting has narrowed catastrophically. April's £6 CPL with 69 leads proves the core offer and market work; you've lost operational grip between May and July.

The cliff doesn't look gradual drift—it's a hard drop June-to-July despite similar spend (£417 → £109), suggesting you either killed a performing audience, paused winning creatives, or platform algorithm penalised low-engagement creative. Your 75% close rate in July is a false comfort; with only 4 leads, that's a lucky streak, not a trend.

**Actions**

1. **Resurrect April's exact setup immediately.** Pull the creative assets, audience segment, bid strategy, and placements from April. Run a controlled test: allocate £150/week to April's proven configuration (lookalike audience, broad interest targeting, video creative if you used it). Document what's different now (creative fatigue, audience saturation, iOS privacy impact?). If April bounces back to £8–10 CPL, you've found the regression point.

2. **Test three new creative angles against a small budget (£60/week).** April's 69 leads came from something—rotate three fresh assets: (a) member transformation testimonial (before/after + voiceover on 6-week outcome), (b) "women-only" angle (privacy, no judgment, community focus), (c) time-pressure hook ("last 5 spots in July cohort"). Use static image + text, not video if video is stale. Run each for 5–7 days, kill underperformers at day 3 if CPL exceeds £15.

3. **Widen audience scope.** June–July creep towards narrow targeting (likely retargeting or tight lookalikes) kills reach.
